subject: Mathematics
q: The number of solutions of the equation, $\sin ^{-1} x=2 \tan ^{-1} x$ (in principal values) is :
question: The number of solutions of the equation, $\sin ^{-1} x=2 \tan ^{-1} x$ (in principal values) is :
solution:
Given equation is
$$
\sin ^{-1} x=2 \tan ^{-1} x
$$
Now, this equation has only one solution.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \text { LHS }=\sin ^{-1} 1=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
& \text { and } \text { RHS }=2 \tan ^{-1} 1=2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
Also, $x=1$ gives angle value as $\frac{\pi}{4}$ and $\frac{5 \pi}{4}$ $\frac{5 \pi}{4}$ is outside the principal value.

solution:
The given equation 2cos2θ+3sinθ=0 can be written as

21-sin2θ+3sinθ=0

⇒2sin2θ-3sinθ-2=0

⇒2sinθ+1sinθ-2=0

⇒sinθ=2, -12, sinθ≠2

⇒sinθ=-12

Hence, solutions in -2π, 2π are
-π+π6, -π6, π+π6, 2π-π6

Sum of the elements is =2π.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following two statements.
Statement $\boldsymbol{p}$ :
The value of $\sin 120^{\circ}$ can be divided by taking $\theta=240^{\circ}$ in the equation
$$
2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2}=\sqrt{1+\sin \theta}-\sqrt{1-\sin \theta} .
$$
Statement $\boldsymbol{q}$ :
The angles $A, B, C$ and $D$ of any quadrilateral $A B C D$ satisfy the equation
$$
\cos \left(\frac{1}{2}(A+C)\right)+\cos \left(\frac{1}{2}(B+D)\right)=0
$$
Then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively.
question:
Consider the following two statements.
Statement $\boldsymbol{p}$ :
The value of $\sin 120^{\circ}$ can be divided by taking $\theta=240^{\circ}$ in the equation
$$
2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2}=\sqrt{1+\sin \theta}-\sqrt{1-\sin \theta} .
$$
Statement $\boldsymbol{q}$ :
The angles $A, B, C$ and $D$ of any quadrilateral $A B C D$ satisfy the equation
$$
\cos \left(\frac{1}{2}(A+C)\right)+\cos \left(\frac{1}{2}(B+D)\right)=0
$$
Then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively.
solution:
Statement $p$ :
$$
\sin 120^{\circ}=\cos 30^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow 2 \sin 120^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}
$$
So, $\sqrt{1+\sin 240^{\circ}}-\sqrt{1-\sin 240^{\circ}}=$
$$
\sqrt{\frac{1-\sqrt{3}}{2}}-\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{3}}{2}} \neq \sqrt{3}
$$
Statement $\mathbf{q}$ :
So, $A+B+C+D=2 \pi$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow \frac{A+C}{2}+\frac{B+D}{2}=\pi \\
&\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{A+C}{2}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{B+D}{2}\right) \\
&=\cos \left(\frac{A+C}{2}\right)-\cos \left(\frac{A+C}{2}\right)=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore, statement $p$ is false and statement $q$ is true.

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then the number of values of <math><mi>x</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is:

solution:
sinx-sin2x+sin3x=0

⇒sinx+sin3x-sin2x=0

⇒2sin2 x .cosx-sin2x=0

⇒sin2x=0 or cosx=12

⇒2x=0 or x=π3

⇒x=0 or  x=π3

∴ Number of solutions =2

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of solutions of equation $(4-\sqrt{3}) \sin x$ $-2 \sqrt{3} \cos ^2 x=-\frac{4}{1+\sqrt{3}}, x \in\left[-2 \pi, \frac{5 \pi}{2}\right]$ is
question: The number of solutions of equation $(4-\sqrt{3}) \sin x$ $-2 \sqrt{3} \cos ^2 x=-\frac{4}{1+\sqrt{3}}, x \in\left[-2 \pi, \frac{5 \pi}{2}\right]$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & (4-\sqrt{3}) \sin x-2 \sqrt{3} \cos ^2 x=\frac{-4}{1+\sqrt{3}}, x \in\left[-2 \pi, \frac{5 \pi}{2}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow(4-\sqrt{3}) \sin x-2 \sqrt{3}\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right)=2(1-\sqrt{3}) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{3} \sin ^2 x+4 \sin x-\sqrt{3} \sin x-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(2 \sin x-1)(\sqrt{3} \sin x+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \therefore \text { Number of solution }=5\end{aligned}$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> solutions in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="⌉" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> belong to :
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> solutions in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="⌉" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> belong to :
solution:
Given: 2sin3x+sin2xcosx+4sinx-4=0

⇒2sin3x+2sinx·cos2x+4sinx-4=0

⇒2sin3x+2sinx·1-sin2x+4sinx-4=0

⇒2sin3x+2sinx-2sin3x+4sinx-4=0

⇒6sinx-4=0

⇒sinx=23

Now, for exactly three solution we get,

⇒n=5 (in the given interval)

So, x2+nx+n-3=0

⇒x2+5x+2=0

⇒x=-5±172

So, the required interval is (-∞,0).

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of elements in the set $\left\{x \in\left[0,180^{\circ}\right]: \tan \left(x+100^{\circ}\right)=\tan \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \tan x \tan \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)\right\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The number of elements in the set $\left\{x \in\left[0,180^{\circ}\right]: \tan \left(x+100^{\circ}\right)=\tan \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \tan x \tan \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)\right\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$\frac{\tan(x+100°)}{\tan x} = \tan(x+50°)\tan(x-50°)$
$\frac{\sin(x+100°)\cos x}{\cos(x+100°)\sin x} = \frac{\sin(x+50°)\sin(x-50°)}{\cos(x+50°)\cos(x-50°)}$
Applying Componendo & Dividendo:
$\frac{\sin(2x+100°)}{\sin 100°} = \frac{\cos 100°}{-\cos 2x}$
$2\sin(2x+100°)\cos 2x + \sin 200° = 0$
$\sin(4x+100°) + \sin 100° + \sin 200° = 0$
$\sin(4x+100°) = -2\sin 150° \cos 50°$
$\sin(4x+100°) = -\cos 50° = \sin(-40°)$
$\therefore 4x + 100° = n\pi + (-1)^n(-40°)$
$x = \frac{n\pi + (-1)^{n+1}(40°) - 100°}{4}$
$\therefore x = 30°, 55°, 120°, 145°$ in $(0, \pi)$
$\therefore$ Number of solutions $= 4$

q: The sum of all values of <math><mi> </mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is

solution:
Given sin22θ+cos42θ=34

Using sin2x+cos2x=1, we get

⇒1-cos22θ+cos42θ=34

Let cos22θ=t

⇒t2-t+14=0

⇒t-122=0

⇒t=12

⇒cos22θ=12

⇒2cos22θ-1=0

Using cos2A=2cos2A-1, we get

⇒cos4θ=0

If cosx=0, then x=2n+1π2, n∈Z.

⇒4θ=2n+1π2

⇒θ=2n+1π8

⇒θ=π8, 3π8 ∈ 0, π2

Hence, the sum of the values of θ is π8+3π8=π2.

question: All the pairs <math><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>y</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, that satisfy the inequality <math><msup><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><msqrt><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></msqrt></mrow></msup><mo>·</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn></math> also satisfy the equation:
solution:
Given inequality is
2sin2⁡x-2sin⁡x+5 ≤4sin2⁡y
⇒sin2⁡x-2sin⁡x+1+4≤2sin2⁡y
⇒sin⁡x-12+4≤2sin2⁡y
∵2sin2⁡y∈0, 2  &  sin⁡x-12+4∈2, 22
∵  Only equality holds
⇒sin⁡x-12+4=2sin2⁡y=2
⇒sin⁡x-12+4=4sin4⁡y=4 
⇒sin2⁡y=1   ⇒  sin⁡y=1   &  sin⁡x-12=0 ⇒sin⁡x=1
∴sin⁡y=sin⁡x

question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo><</mo><mi>π</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>y</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
cosx+cosy-cosx+y=32

2cosx+y2·cosx-y2-2cos2x+y2-1=32

2cosx+y2·cosx-y2-2cos2x+y2+1=32

2cosx+y2·cosx-y2-2cos2x+y2=12

4cosx+y2·cosx-y2-4cos2x+y2=1

⇒4cosx+y2·cosx-y2-4cos2x+y2=1=sin2x-y2+cos2x-y2

⇒4cos2x+y2+cos2x-y2-4cosx+y2·cosx-y2+sin2x-y2=0

⇒2cosx+y2-cosx-y22+sin2x-y2=0

⇒sinx-y2=0 and cosx+y2=12cosx-y2

⇒x=y and cosx=12=cosy

∴ sinx=32

⇒sinx+cosy=1+32

option 4

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then the number of elements in the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>tan</mi><mi>θ</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mi>tan</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mi>tan</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is _____ .
solution:
Given,

 S=-π,π2--π2,-π4,-3π4,π4 and the set A=θ∈S:tanθ1+5tan2θ=5-tan2θ

Now let tanα=5

So, tanθ1+5tan2θ=5-tan2θ becomes,

⇒tanθ1+tanαtan2θ=tanα-tan2θ

On rearranging we get,

⇒tanθ=tanα-tan2θ1+tanαtan2θ

On using formula tanA-B=tanA-tanB1-tanAtanB we get,

⇒tanθ=tanα-2θ

⇒α-2θ=nπ+θ

⇒  3θ=α-nπ

⇒  θ=α3-nπ3; n∈Z

If θ∈[-π,π/2) then n=0,1,2,3,4 are acceptable solution

So, total 5 solutions are possible.

question: If the sum of solutions of the system of equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mi>π</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to _______.
solution:
Given,

2sin2θ-cos2θ=0

⇒2sin2θ-1-2sin2θ=0

⇒sin2θ=122

⇒θ=π6,5π6,7π6,11π6

Also given, 2cos2θ+3sinθ=0

⇒2sin2θ-3sinθ-2=0

⇒ sinθ=-12

⇒θ=7π6,11π6

So, the common solution is

θ=7π6,11π6

Sum =7π+11π6=3π=kπ

⇒K=3

q: The number of solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>;</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
question: The number of solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>;</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
Given: 4sin2x-4cos3x+9-4cosx=0

⇒4-4cos2x-4cos3x+9-4cosx=0

⇒4cos3x+4cos2x+4cosx-13=0

⇒4cos3x+4cos2x+4cosx=13

We know that, -1≤cosx≤1

So, the maximum value of LHS is 4+4+4=12

But, RHS is 13.

So, no solutions are possible for the given equation.

q: The number of solutions of $\sin ^2 x+\left(2+2 x-x^2\right) \sin x-3(x-1)^2=0$, where $-\pi \leq x \leq \pi$, is________
question: The number of solutions of $\sin ^2 x+\left(2+2 x-x^2\right) \sin x-3(x-1)^2=0$, where $-\pi \leq x \leq \pi$, is________
solution:
$\sin ^2 x-\left(x^2-2 x-2\right) \sin x-3(x-1)^2=0$
$\begin{aligned} & \sin x=-3(\text { reject }) \text { or }(x-1)^2 \\ & \sin x=(x-1)^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\theta \in\left[-\frac{7 \pi}{6}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}\right]$, then the number of solutions of $\sqrt{3} \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta-2(\sqrt{3}-1) \operatorname{cosec} \theta-4=0$, is equal to
question: If $\theta \in\left[-\frac{7 \pi}{6}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}\right]$, then the number of solutions of $\sqrt{3} \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta-2(\sqrt{3}-1) \operatorname{cosec} \theta-4=0$, is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \operatorname{cosec} \theta=\frac{2(\sqrt{3}-1) \pm \sqrt{4(3+1-2 \sqrt{3})+16 \sqrt{3}}}{2 \sqrt{3}} \\ & =\frac{2(\sqrt{3-1}) \pm \sqrt{16+8 \sqrt{3}}}{2 \sqrt{3}} \\ & =\frac{2(\sqrt{3}-1) \pm(2+2 \sqrt{3})}{2 \sqrt{3}}\end{aligned}$
$\operatorname{cosec} \theta=2$ or $\frac{-2}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\therefore \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}$ or $\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\therefore \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}$ has 3 solutions \& also $\sin \theta=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}$
has 3 solutions in $\left[\frac{-7 \pi}{6}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}\right]$

q: The number of distinct real roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is:
question: The number of distinct real roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is:
solution:
We have, sinxcosxcosxcosxsinxcosxcosxcosxsinx=0,-π4≤x≤π4

Apply: R1→R1-R2 and R2→R2-R3

sinx-cosxcosx-sinx00sinx-cosxcosx-sinxcosxcosxsinx=0

⇒sinx-cosx21-1001-1cosxcosxsinx=0

⇒sinx-cosx2sinx+2cosx=0

⇒sinx=cosx or sinx=-2cosx

∴ x=π4

Since, -π4≤x≤π4, we get -1≤tanx≤1

⇒tanx=1⇒x=π4

Hence, number of roots is one.

Then <math><mi>S</mi></math> is equal to:
question: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math> be the set of all <math><mi>α</mi><mtext> </mtext><mo>∈</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>R</mi></math> such that the equation, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></math> has a solution. Then <math><mi>S</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
The given equation can be written as:
1-2sin2⁡x+ α sinx=2α-7
⇒2sin2⁡x-αsin⁡x+2α-8=0
⇒sinx=α-4 2,  2 sinx ≠2
For at least one solution
-1≤α-42 ≤1
⇒α∈[2,6]

subject: Mathematics
q: The equation $e^{\sin x}-e^{-\sin x}-4=0$ has
question: The equation $e^{\sin x}-e^{-\sin x}-4=0$ has
solution:
$e^{\sin x}-e^{-\sin x}=4 \quad \Rightarrow e^{\sin x}=t$
$t-\frac{1}{t}=4$
$\begin{array}{ll}t^2-4 t-1=0 & \Rightarrow t=\frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16+4}}{2} \\ \Rightarrow t=\frac{4 \pm 2 \sqrt{5}}{2} & \Rightarrow t=2 \pm \sqrt{5}\end{array}$
$e^{\sin x}=2 \pm \sqrt{5} \quad \quad-1 \leq \sin x \leq 1 \quad \quad \frac{1}{e} \leq e^{\sin x} \leq e$
$e^{\sin x}=2+\sqrt{5}$ not possible 
$e^{\sin x}=2-\sqrt{5}$ not possible
$\therefore$ hence no solution

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $S=\left\{\sin ^2 2 \theta:\left(\sin ^4 \theta+\cos ^4 \theta\right) x^2+(\sin 2 \theta) x+\left(\sin ^6 \theta+\cos ^6 \theta\right)=0\right.$ has real roots $\}$. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the smallest and largest elements of the set $S$, respectively, then $3\left((\alpha-2)^2+(\beta-1)^2\right)$ equals _________
question: Let $S=\left\{\sin ^2 2 \theta:\left(\sin ^4 \theta+\cos ^4 \theta\right) x^2+(\sin 2 \theta) x+\left(\sin ^6 \theta+\cos ^6 \theta\right)=0\right.$ has real roots $\}$. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the smallest and largest elements of the set $S$, respectively, then $3\left((\alpha-2)^2+(\beta-1)^2\right)$ equals _________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & D=(\sin 2 \theta)^2-4\left(1-\frac{\sin ^2 2 \theta}{2}\right)\left(1-\frac{3}{4} \sin ^2 2 \theta\right) \\ & =(\sin 2 \theta)^2-4\left(1-\frac{5}{4} \sin ^2 2 \theta+\frac{3}{8} \sin ^4 2 \theta\right) \\ & D=-\frac{3}{2} \sin ^4 2 \theta+6 \sin ^2 2 \theta-4>0 \\ & 3 \sin ^4 2 \theta-12 \sin ^2 2 \theta+8 < 0 \\ & \sin ^2 2 \theta=\frac{12 \pm \sqrt{12^2-12.8}}{6}=\frac{12 \pm 4 \sqrt{3}}{6}=\frac{6 \pm 2 \sqrt{3}}{3} \\ & \sin ^2 2 \theta=2 \pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}, \text { but } \sin ^2 2 \theta \in[0,1] \\ & \therefore \alpha=2-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}, \beta=1 \rightarrow(\alpha-2)^2=\frac{4}{3},(\beta-1)^2=0 \\ & 3(\alpha-2)^2+(\beta-1)^2=4\end{aligned}$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is the number of solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is the sum of all these solutions, then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>S</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is :

solution:
2cosx4sinπ4+xsinπ4-x-1=1

⇒  2cosx2cos2x-2cosπ2-1=1

⇒  2cosx2.2cos2x-1-1=1

⇒ 2cosx4cos2x-3=1

⇒ 4cos3x-3cosx=12

⇒ cos3x=12

⇒ 3x=π3,5π3,7π3

⇒ x=π9,5π9,7π9

No. of solutions =n=3

Sum of solutions =S=π9+5π9+7π9=13π9

q: The number of roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mn>81</mn></mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>81</mn></mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to :

solution:
81sin2x+81cos2x=30

81sin2x+81181sin2x=30

81sin2x=t

t+81t=30

t2-30t+81=0

t-3t-27=0

81sin2x=31 or  81sin2x=33

34sin2x=31  or   34sin2x=33

sin2x=14  or  sin2x=34

  

Hence, total 4 solution.

question: If $A=\sin ^2 x+\cos ^4 x$, then for all real $x$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $S = \{\theta \in (-2\pi, 2\pi) : \cos\theta + 1 = \sqrt{3}\sin\theta\}$. Then $\sum_{\theta \in S}\theta$ is equal to:
question: Let $S = \{\theta \in (-2\pi, 2\pi) : \cos\theta + 1 = \sqrt{3}\sin\theta\}$. Then $\sum_{\theta \in S}\theta$ is equal to:
solution:
Given equation: $\cos\theta + 1 = \sqrt{3}\sin\theta$

Using half-angle formulas, we get:

$2\cos^2\dfrac{\theta}{2} = 2\sqrt{3}\sin\dfrac{\theta}{2}\cos\dfrac{\theta}{2}$

$2\cos\dfrac{\theta}{2} \left( \cos\dfrac{\theta}{2} - \sqrt{3}\sin\dfrac{\theta}{2} \right) = 0$

This gives two cases:

Case 1: $\cos\dfrac{\theta}{2} = 0$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\theta}{2} = (2k+1)\dfrac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = (2k+1)\pi$

Since $\theta \in (-2\pi, 2\pi)$, the possible values are $\theta = -\pi, \pi$.

Case 2: $\cos\dfrac{\theta}{2} - \sqrt{3}\sin\dfrac{\theta}{2} = 0$

$\Rightarrow \tan\dfrac{\theta}{2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\theta}{2} = k\pi + \dfrac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow \theta = 2k\pi + \dfrac{\pi}{3}$

Since $\theta \in (-2\pi, 2\pi)$, the possible values are:

For $k = 0$, $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$

For $k = -1$, $\theta = -2\pi + \dfrac{\pi}{3} = -\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

Thus, the set of solutions is $S = \left\{-\pi, \pi, \dfrac{\pi}{3}, -\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right\}$.

Sum of all solutions in $S$:

$\sum_{\theta \in S}\theta = -\pi + \pi + \dfrac{\pi}{3} - \dfrac{5\pi}{3} = -\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

Answer: $-\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

subject: Mathematics
q: A value of $x$ for which $\sin \left(\cot ^{-1}(1+x)\right)=\cos$ $\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$, is :
question: A value of $x$ for which $\sin \left(\cot ^{-1}(1+x)\right)=\cos$ $\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$, is :
solution:
$\sin \left(\cot ^{-1}(1+x)\right)=\cos \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \operatorname{cosec}^2\left(\cot ^{-1}(1+x)\right)=\sec ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 1+\left[\cot \left(\cot ^{-1}(1+x)\right)\right]^2 \\ & \Rightarrow=1+\left[\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)\right]^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(1+x)^2=x^2 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
The number of solutions of the equation
$\cos 2 \theta \cos \frac{\theta}{2}+\cos \frac{5 \theta}{2}=2 \cos ^3 \frac{5 \theta}{2}$
in $\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ is :
question:
The number of solutions of the equation
$\cos 2 \theta \cos \frac{\theta}{2}+\cos \frac{5 \theta}{2}=2 \cos ^3 \frac{5 \theta}{2}$
in $\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \cos 2 \theta \cos \frac{\theta}{2}+\cos \frac{5 \theta}{2}=2 \cos ^3 \frac{5 \theta}{2} \\ & \frac{1}{2}\left(2 \cos 2 \theta \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)+\cos \frac{50}{2} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\cos \frac{15 \theta}{2}+3 \cos \frac{5 \theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$
or solving
$\begin{aligned}
& \cos \frac{3 \theta}{2}=\cos \frac{15 \theta}{2} \\ & \cos \frac{15 \theta}{2}-\cos \frac{3 \theta}{2}=0 \\ & 2 \sin 30 \sin \frac{9 \theta}{2}=0
\end{aligned}$
$3 \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi \text { or } \frac{9 \theta}{2}=\mathrm{m} \pi$
$\begin{aligned}
& \theta=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{3} \quad \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{~m} \pi}{9} \\ & \theta=\left\{-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{3}, 0\right\} \\ & \theta=\left\{-\frac{4 \pi}{9}, \frac{-2 \pi}{9}, \frac{4 \pi}{9}, \frac{2 \pi}{9}\right\}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>8</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>8</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mfenced><mi>S</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><munder><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></mrow></munder><mfenced><mrow><mi>sec</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>cosec</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>8</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>8</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mfenced><mi>S</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><munder><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></mrow></munder><mfenced><mrow><mi>sec</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>cosec</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given, 82sin2θ+82cos2θ=16

⇒82sin2θ+82-2sin2θ=16

Now let 82sin2θ=y 

⇒y+64y=16

⇒  y=8

⇒82sin2θ=8

⇒sin2θ=12

⇒θ∈π4,3π4,5π4,7π4

Now nS+∑θ∈Ssecπ4+2θcosecπ4+2θ

 =nS+∑θ∈S1cosπ4+2θsinπ4+2θ

=4+∑θ∈S22cosπ4+2θsinπ4+2θ

=4+∑θ∈S2cosecπ2+4θ

=4+2cosecπ2+π+2cosecπ2+3π+2cosecπ2+5π+2cosecπ2+7π

=4+2-cosecπ2-cosecπ2-cosecπ2-cosecπ2

=4+-2×4=-4
